message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.6},{"player":"RABBIT","probability":0.4}],"behavioral_basis":["Contributed the full endowment every round (6/6), matching a strongly prosocial, consistent cooperative strategy aligned with high agreeableness and conscientiousness.","High ultimatum and trust-game generosity in my profile suggests I'd reliably cooperate rather than free-ride; CHICKEN and RABBIT show that behavior.","High social desirability and aspiration to be remembered as helpful predict steady contributions to the public good rather than intermittent cooperation or opportunistic defection."],"uncertainty_notes":["CHICKEN and RABBIT have identical contribution patterns, so distinguishing between them from behavior alone is impossible—hence split probability.","No private motives or messages from either player are available (only PARROT messaged), so inferred alignment relies solely on contribution patterns.","Binary choice format (0 or 20) and small number of rounds limit inference; real-world personality could produce similar behavior for different reasons (norm adherence, experimenter effects, or payoff-maximization under beliefs about others)."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across rounds (SLOTH) match high agreeableness, high conscientiousness, and pro‑social economic choices (generous ultimatum/trust/dictator behavior).","Minimal, friendly chat from SLOTH (brief agreement, polite farewell) fits a moderately introverted, low‑neurotic, high‑communion profile that acts cooperatively without seeking attention.","GORILLA explicitly states 'i'm just giving 20 and not punishing' and repeatedly contributed 20 — aligns with a non‑punitive, reliable cooperator consistent with the persona's empathy and low punishment tendency.","DUCK consistently contributed and defended their record ('I'm +20 every time') showing dependable cooperation and modest assertiveness; slightly more willing to punish once when others appeared to defect, which makes DUCK a plausible but weaker match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral inferences come from a short, noisy interaction — punishments and chat may reflect momentary emotions rather than stable personality.","System display inconsistencies (players disputing reported contributions) complicate interpretation of motives and who truly contributed each round.","High social desirability in the persona could mean public messages overstate cooperativeness; conversely low self‑monitoring suggests behavior may not be finely tuned to impression management — these mixed signals add uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.15},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently high contributions (always 20) match my high agreeableness, strong communal values, and generous behavior in other economic games (ultimatum, trust, dictator).","High conscientiousness and social-desirability suggest I would behave reliably and prosocially across rounds rather than fluctuate or retaliate, which aligns with SLOTH’s steady cooperation.","Low neuroticism and caregiving self-descriptions (aspiration to help, actual role caring for mother) make sudden spiteful zero contributions unlikely; GORILLA’s conditional/cohort behavior is a secondary match, while DUCK’s late defection is least consistent with my profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["I don’t see players’ messages or payoffs, so some contribution changes could reflect responses to information I don’t have (which could make conditional strategies more plausible).","Self-report measures (social desirability, stated goals) may overstate actual in-game behavior, so I may be somewhat less extreme than a perfect cooperator.","Limited rounds and small group dynamics can induce strategic changes (end-game effects) that could push even generally cooperative people toward non-cooperative moves in the final round."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.75},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.25}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, empathy, and past generosity in economic games (ultimatum, dictator, trust) predict consistently high contributions — matches GORILLA's constant 20/20 behavior.","Strong conscientiousness and communal orientation suggest regular, reliable prosocial behavior; SLOTH's mostly-high contributions (four rounds of 20) align but occasional lower contributions (0 and 10) could reflect real-life constraints (caretaking responsibilities) or strategic adjustments.","Low neuroticism and high social desirability make dramatic spiteful actions (punishing cooperators or persistent low contributions) unlikely — DUCK's low early contributions and the lone punishment of a full contributor are inconsistent with the persona."],"uncertainty_notes":["High social desirability score means self-reported traits/answers may overstate prosocial tendencies.","Punishment motives and private constraints are unobserved; occasional reduced contributions (as with SLOTH) could be situational rather than dispositional.","Small number of rounds and limited behavioral signals (no messages shown) make inferences probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across rounds (cooperative, generous behavior).","Frequent, broad rewarding of contributors (prosocial, fairness-motivated).","Chat messages endorsing rewarding cooperators and admonishing free-riders (voices social norms and encouragement).","Behavior matches high agreeableness, high conscientiousness, strong prosocial/other-oriented economic choices (ultimatum, trust, dictator behaviours)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions are limited to a specific game context and may not capture private motives.","High social-desirability score in the profile means stated/observed prosociality could reflect desire to appear kind rather than underlying preference.","Several players show similar cooperative patterns (GORILLA, DUCK, SLOTH, CROCODILE), so assignment is probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.65},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.25},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions and frequent small positive rewards (CHICK) match high agreeableness, high generosity (ultimatum/dictator offers), and communal orientation in the profile.","Pro-social chat promoting cooperation and steady rewarding with limited/occasional small punishments (SLOTH) aligns with high conscientiousness, empathy, and norm-enforcement without vindictiveness.","Moderate consistent contributions with occasional small punishments of non-contributors (DUCK) fits a conscientious, norm-enforcing cooperator though less actively rewarding than CHICK, making it a weaker match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Player actions are only observed in this specific game context and may reflect strategic or situational motives rather than stable personality.","High social-desirability in the profile suggests stated preferences that could differ from revealed behavior; some players may be performing for the group chat audience.","Punishment actions vary in magnitude and may reflect reactions by multiple people; attribution of motives (e.g., empathy vs. strategic sanctioning) is inferential and uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.55},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.3},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions: Gorilla, Sloth, and Duck (after early rounds) contributed 20 each round most of the time, matching the respondent's high agreeableness, generosity (ultimatum/dictator giving), and strong conscientiousness.","Gorilla's messages are cooperative and mildly analytical (e.g., 'If everyone contributed...'; calculating payoff later). This fits the respondent's combination of high need for cognition, moderate numeracy, and preference for explaining why cooperation is beneficial.","Sloth's minimal chat, steady contributions, and pragmatic question ('how many rounds') align with the respondent's lower extraversion, high conscientiousness, and practical/steady cooperative behavior.","Duck's friendly, supportive tone (hearts, encouragement) matches the respondent's empathic and kind orientation, but Duck initially defected which is less consistent with the respondent's high pro-sociality and generous game behavior; thus Duck is a weaker match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only limited chat and contribution traces are available — several players display overlapping cooperative behavior, so exact identification is uncertain.","Some messages (e.g., Parrot's blunt language or Duck's early defection) could reflect momentary moods or jokes rather than stable personality, making alignment imprecise.","Demographic and internal trait information may not map perfectly to brief online behavior; social desirability may have influenced the respondent's likely on-record actions."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.25},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent high contributions: DUCK and CHICKEN contribute in most rounds, matching the person’s communal, fair, and prosocial tendencies (high agreeableness, high communion, generous ultimatum/trust/dictator behavior).","Low-to-moderate punishment: DUCK sends only occasional, small punishments (fits a high-empathy, high-social-desirability person who enforces fairness lightly rather than aggressive punishment). PARROT is also frequently cooperative and punishes infrequently, consistent with a kind, non-confrontational style.","Quiet leadership and caretaking orientation: The persona emphasizes helping others and conscientious responsibility; DUCK’s pattern of steady cooperation with only occasional corrective punishment aligns with a reliable, caretaking style rather than the heavy, retaliatory punishment exhibited by SLOTH or DOG."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe in-game contributions/punishments and limited chat; real-life personality inferences are indirect and may be noisy.","Some cooperative players (e.g., CHICKEN) show occasional punishments early on that could reflect context-specific retaliation rather than dispositional hostility; this makes exact matching uncertain.","Social desirability in the persona may produce behavior that looks cooperative in public settings but could diverge in private decisions not captured here."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"COW","probability":0.55},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.30},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent high contributions (mostly 20) and frequent supportive/chatty messages match the profile's high agreeableness, communal orientation, and social-desirability—COW repeatedly affirms the group and signs off warmly.","Large, broad reward-giving (GORILLA) and generally cooperative contributions align with the profile's high generosity (ultimatum/trust behavior), strong conscientiousness, and preference for cooperative outcomes; GORILLA's mass rewards look like prosocial enforcement of the group's norm.","SLOTH's modest early contribution then steady cooperation plus authentic, low-performance banter (e.g., admitting slowness with math) fits the profile's low self-monitoring, moderate extraversion, high conscientiousness (committing to the group), and tendency toward frank, caring remarks."],"uncertainty_notes":["Many players exhibited nearly identical contribution behavior (widespread 20s), so distinguishing on contribution alone is noisy.","Chat style and occasional misclicks make it hard to be certain who best matches the respondent's background (age, regional cues, caregiving orientation are not explicit in chat).","Reward behavior is a strong signal of generosity but could reflect strategic signaling; social desirability may cause players to over-affirm in chat, complicating inference."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"OWL","probability":0.6},{"player":"MONKEY","probability":0.3},{"player":"SNAKE","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across rounds (OWL always contributed 20) match high conscientiousness and cooperative orientation.","OWL's messages ('We will all be better off if we all contribute the max', 'Thank you everyone :)') are polite, communal, and encouraging, aligning with high agreeableness, empathy, and caregiving values.","MONKEY consistently pushed for cooperation, discouraged punishment ('punishing just makes everyone lose coins'), and behaved enthusiastically and supportive — fits a cooperative, non-punitive, moderately sociable player consistent with this profile.","SNAKE provided analytic reasoning about payoffs and repeatedly urged max contributions (matches higher need for cognition and conscientiousness), but used threats/punishments which conflicts with the high agreeableness/empathy, so SNAKE is a partial match.","No top match used heavy self-promotion or unpredictable selfish play; the selected players are those promoting group welfare and steady contribution, which best fits the respondent's communal, conscientious, and prosocial pattern."],"uncertainty_notes":["Player identities (age/gender/background) are unknown — matching is based solely on observed in-game behavior and chat content, which may not reflect underlying personality fully.","Several players displayed similar cooperative behavior (many contributed 20 and encouraged others), so distinguishing among them from chat volume/tone is imprecise.","High social-desirability score in the respondent and possible strategic signaling in the game mean the respondent might have behaved more or less punitively than their true inclinations; this introduces additional uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.60},{"player":"DOG","probability":0.25},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness and strong communal orientation — CHICKEN rewarded many other players repeatedly and often contributed highly in later rounds, matching the persona's strong prosocial and fairness concerns.","High conscientiousness and dependable cooperation — DOG contributed near-maximum in most rounds, agreed to the group plan in chat, and gave occasional rewards, reflecting the persona's disciplined, caretaker tendencies and preference for cooperation.","Coordination and positive reinforcement — PARROT initiated the plan to contribute all in final rounds and provided encouraging messages (\"Y'all amazing\"), consistent with the persona's tendency to promote group cohesion and offer social support."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inferences are based only on observed contributions, rewards, and chat — motives (e.g., genuine altruism vs. strategic signaling) are not directly observable.","Some players (e.g., DUCK, SLOTH) show mixed signals (heavy rewarding but inconsistent contributions, or high contributions but low visible rewarding), which could also map partly to aspects of the persona; assignment reflects best overall fit, not certainty.","High social-desirability in the persona and in-game behavior may cause both observed and self-reported prosocial actions to be amplified, so matches are probabilistic rather than definitive."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.7},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent high contributions: SLOTH, DUCK, and GORILLA repeatedly contribute the full 20, matching the subject's high conscientiousness, high communal orientation, and generosity in ultimatum/dictator/trust games.","Norm enforcement: SLOTH loudly advocates full contributions and repeatedly incurs personal cost to punish low contributors (especially DOG and CHICKEN), consistent with a duty-focused, conscientious caretaker who enforces fairness despite personal cost (matches high secondary conscientiousness, mental-accounting focus, and strong social-desirability/ fairness motives).","Supportive communication and selective reciprocity: DUCK agrees with cooperative norms, communicates, and both punishes defectors moderately and rewards cooperators (large positive transfers in round 18), aligning with the subject's high agreeableness, empathy, and tendency to reward/ trust others.","Quiet reliable cooperator: GORILLA contributes fully but stays mostly silent and does little punishment or reward, matching the subject's low extraversion but strong reliability; included as a minor match for the subject's steadiness and low-self monitoring."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed punishment intensity (especially SLOTH's very large punishments) is extreme relative to typical high-agreeableness behavior; the subject's high agreeableness makes extreme, repeated harsh punishment somewhat uncertain—could reflect norm-enforcement motivation rather than pure hostility.","Behavioral signals may be strategic or experiment-specific (e.g., aiming to influence bonus thresholds); messaging and unseen motives limit certainty about which actions reflect stable personality.","Only in-game actions are available; demographic and qualitative cues (caretaking duty, desire to help) informed the match but cannot fully disambiguate between similarly cooperative players."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"COW","probability":0.55},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.30},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across all 14 rounds (COW and SLOTH each contributed every round) match the respondent's high agreeableness, high conscientiousness, and duty-oriented caretaker self-description.","COW's pattern of giving small, broad rewards to many players each round fits the respondent's high communion/empathy, moderate generosity (ultimatum/dictator behavior), and high social-desirability — steady, modest prosocial acts rather than sporadic large gestures.","SLOTH's consistent contributions plus targeted larger rewards align with the respondent's strong rule-following/conscientious streak and desire to help and be seen as kind (aspirational hospice volunteering).","PARROT shows high cooperation and frequent rewarding in early rounds but is slightly less consistent (one round of non‑contribution), matching the respondent's generally prosocial but practical, not risk-taking profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inferring motives from only contribution/reward actions is imprecise — identical actions can reflect different motives (inequity aversion, signaling, reciprocity, experimenter demand).","High social-desirability score suggests reported aspirations and some behavior may be influenced by wanting to appear kind; observed in-game rewards might partly reflect that rather than pure preference.","Players' identities and private rationales are unobserved; matching is based on behavioral patterns (consistency, reward size/distribution) that are only proxies for the supplied personality measures."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICK","probability":0.6},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25},{"player":"RABBIT","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent full contributions across rounds and frequent encouraging group chat (e.g. “come on everyone”, “we got this”) match high agreeableness, communion, generosity, and conscientiousness in the profile.","CHICK often called out defectors and reminded group goals, reflecting a prosocial, fairness-oriented stance consistent with high ultimatum/ dictator generosity and high trust-game sending in the profile.","CHICKEN’s repeated encouragement, celebratory messages, and apology for an accidental missed contribution fit a cooperative, conscientious, and socially engaged player.","RABBIT’s steady full contributions and supportive comments (e.g. ‘i been max since the start’) align with a reliable, communal contributor, though with less visible leadership than CHICK."],"uncertainty_notes":["All strong contributors displayed similar behavior (many players contributed max nearly every round), so distinguishing among them from actions alone is imprecise.","Chat style and occasional brief messages provide limited demographic cues; matching to the detailed persona (age, gender, life role) is inferential and uncertain.","Social desirability and low self-monitoring in the profile create mixed signals; choices may reflect both authentic cooperation and desire to appear prosocial, which can’t be disentangled from observed behavior."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.65},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.25},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, empathy, and social-desirability in the profile match CHICKEN's repeated chat messages emphasizing kindness, not wanting to punish, and group-minded statements (\"we are all in this together\").","Strong conscientiousness and steady contribution behavior (quiet but cooperative) align with GORILLA, who contributes reliably and posts minimal, pragmatic chat — matching low extraversion and high conscientiousness.","PARROT also promotes cooperation and praises group successes (and occasionally enforces norms), which fits the profile's combination of cooperation and a willingness to support group norms without being overly punitive."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inferred matches rely on short chat excerpts and contribution patterns; multiple players show cooperative behavior so identification is probabilistic.","Social-desirability and self-report measures may bias expressed preferences (e.g., saying \"I don't want to punish anyone\") versus actual punitive choices, so behavioral match is uncertain.","Some players show mixed behavior (occasional lapses, punishments) that could also be consistent with the profile; probabilities reflect relative rather than definitive confidence."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DOG","probability":0.5},{"player":"MOOSE","probability":0.3},{"player":"OWL","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently cooperative behavior: DOG and MOOSE contributed every round (10/10), matching the subject's high agreeableness, empathy, and tendency toward fairness (high ultimatum and dictator offers).","Conscientiousness and caregiving orientation: The subject's strong conscientiousness and stated caregiving role suggest reliably contributing each round rather than opportunistic defection—traits reflected in the unvarying contributors (DOG, MOOSE).","Preference for appearing fair / socially desirable: High social desirability score makes frequent cooperation likely; OWL (9/10 contributions) is included as a near-constant cooperator consistent with this tendency.","Generosity and trust: High trust-game sending and generous ultimatum offers align with steady public-good contributions rather than strategic free-riding, supporting selection of consistent cooperators."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavior is inferred from contribution patterns only; names (animal labels) provide no demographic cues, so mapping is approximate.","High social desirability may have influenced reported attitudes and game decisions; observed actions could still reflect experiment-specific strategies rather than stable real-world behavior.","Multiple players show similar high-cooperation patterns (e.g., FROG, WHALE also always cooperated), so assignment among perfect cooperators is somewhat arbitrary based on non-behavioral persona cues."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.7},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.2},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently contributed full amount after an initial accidental slip and actively encouraged others to cooperate — matches high agreeableness, communion, empathy, and social-desirability tendencies to be helpful and cooperative.","Sent apologetic/explanatory messages about a control mistake and then persisted in coordinating the group — consistent with conscientiousness and desire to present oneself positively.","Willingness to communicate team-oriented messages (encouragement, updates, ‘good teamwork’) fits the person’s stated aspiration to be remembered as kind and helpful and the high scores on ultimatum/trust/dictator generosity.","CHICKEN’s quiet but cooperative behavior (contributed fully, minimal chat) aligns with moderate extraversion paired with high agreeableness and conscientiousness.","GORILLA’s cooperative but pragmatic/curious stance (questions about the task, factual updates) could reflect the participant’s practical, lower-openness tendencies, but is less aligned with the strongly communal/empathetic profile than DUCK."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavior is inferred from limited public chat and contribution choices; private motivations aren’t observable.","High social-desirability score suggests the person might behave more cooperatively in visible settings than privately, which could make matching participants ambiguous.","Some observed acts (e.g., DUCK’s early mistake) could be genuine slips or role signals; interpretation depends on whether one treats them as accidental vs. strategic.","Demographics (region, age, politics) provide limited signal for in-game behavior compared with personality measures, so matches are probabilistic rather than certain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.75},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.18},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.07}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, high communal values, generous ultimatum/dictator/trust choices — predicts a tendency to contribute rather than free-ride; SLOTH is the most frequent contributor observed (6 contributions) so best matches a generally prosocial profile.","Low neuroticism, high conscientiousness and strong self-concept clarity — suggests consistent, norm-following behavior rather than attention-seeking chat; SLOTH contributed repeatedly but did not chat, fitting a steady contributor who isn’t highly performative.","Moderate extraversion and occasional cooperation in the data — GORILLA and CHICKEN both chatted and contributed occasionally; these players are plausible secondary matches given some sociality and occasional cooperation."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral trajectories are noisy — all four players mostly defected and contributions were intermittent, so matching is based on relative frequency rather than clear, consistent cooperation.","No punishments and limited chat content reduce visibility into motivations (fairness vs image vs contingent strategies).","High social desirability in the profile could mean stated preferences diverge from actual anonymous behavior, adding uncertainty to the mapping."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.65},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.25},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["SLOTH shows steady, moderately high contributions (≈15–18) and virtually no punitive actions — matches my high agreeableness, high conscientiousness, consistent giving, and low propensity to punish or retaliate.","CHICKEN contributes very high amounts (often 20) and is passive on punishment/reward — aligns with my strong communal orientation, empathy, social-desirability-driven prosociality, and desire to be remembered as kind.","GORILLA actively spends resources to reward others (consistent prosocial reinforcement), which fits my communal/helping values and social-desirability, but Gorillas lower and more variable own contributions (including very low rounds) make this a weaker match to my typical consistent-contributor profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral motives (strategic vs. norm-driven) are not observable — identical actions can reflect different internal motives.","I rely on aggregated trait summaries; individual noisy rounds (e.g., one low-contribution round) could reflect mistakes or experiment-specific strategies that obscure true preference fit.","High social-desirability in my profile could push me toward visible prosocial acts (rewarding) or steady generous contributions — both CHICKEN and GORILLA show facets of that, increasing classification uncertainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.8},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.18},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.02}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness (87th percentile), strong prosocial choices in other games (ultimatum offer 60%, trust sender 80%, dictator 40%) — aligns with consistently high contributions.","High conscientiousness (77th percentile and secondary conscientiousness 100th) and caregiving responsibilities suggest steady, duty-driven cooperation like SLOTH's frequent 15–20 contributions.","Low neuroticism and high self-concept clarity indicate persistence and adherence to social norms even when others defect, matching SLOTH's mostly consistent generosity rather than the steady low contributions of GORILLA."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed actions don’t reveal motives — a high contributor could be signaling or conforming to perceived expectations (social desirability is high).","Single extreme deviations (SLOTH’s zero in round 15, DUCK’s drop to zeros from round 13) could reflect retaliation or experiment specifics; the actual person might react differently to perceived free-riding.","Behavior in one experiment may not fully capture real-world caregiving-driven generosity; probabilities reflect plausibility, not certainty."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.8},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["Almost-perfect, repeated full contributions (20 coins) across most rounds — consistent with high agreeableness, strong conscientiousness, and generous ultimatum/dictator/trust tendencies reported in the profile.","Absence of punishment actions aligns with a cooperative, non-retaliatory orientation and high empathy/social desirability.","Occasional small drops (two rounds of 5) are plausible lapses or situational exceptions for an otherwise rule-following, caretaking personality (matches high self-concept clarity and duty to family).","Gorilla’s pattern (several 10s) shows more frequent compromise/conditional cooperation, which fits less well with the profile’s high generosity and extreme conscientiousness than Sloth’s largely unwavering contributions."],"uncertainty_notes":["Inference is based only on revealed contribution patterns and no communication; identical choices could arise from different motives (e.g., strategic vs. altruistic).","No punishment data provides limited insight into enforcement preferences; social-desirability bias in the subject could inflate cooperative behavior.","The small number of low contributions by the top match introduce ambiguity — they might reflect fatigue, experimentation, or error rather than a different underlying strategy."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.6},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.3},{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistent high contributions: DUCK contributes at or near the maximum in most rounds, matching my high agreeableness, generosity (ultimatum/dictator giving), and conscientious, prosocial orientation.","Reinforcement behavior: GORILLA gives small rewards to high contributors (SLOTH, CHICKEN), which aligns with my fairness sensitivity and tendency to positively reinforce cooperative behavior, even if overall contributions are more moderate.","Inconsistency/conditional cooperation: SLOTH shows strong early cooperation but large swings and eventual free-riding, which is less consistent with my high conscientiousness and social-desirability–driven tendency to maintain cooperative behavior; therefore SLOTH is a weaker match."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed play is limited to 10 rounds and monetary choices; motives (caretaking, signaling, mistake) are not directly observable.","Only two small reward actions were recorded, limiting inference about propensity to reward or punish.","My self-report (high social desirability, high generosity) could map to different strategic behaviors depending on expectations about others; mapping to observed players is probabilistic, not certain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.78},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.12},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, communion, and empathy in the profile predict consistent, prosocial contributions and a preference for rewarding/coaxing cooperation rather than aggressive punishment — Gorilla consistently contributes and issues small rewards to others each round.","High conscientiousness and strong self-concept clarity suggest stable, repeatable behavior across rounds; Gorilla’s steady contribution levels match that consistency.","Profile responses emphasize kindness, helping others, and fairness (ultimatum/dictator generosity, trust behavior). Gorilla’s pattern of rewarding others (including low contributors) aligns with a forgiving, helping orientation. CHICKEN is a weaker match because of inconsistent contributions and lack of active rewarding/punishing; DUCK is a weaker match because the escalating, severe punishments conflict with the high-agreeableness/low-neuroticism profile."],"uncertainty_notes":["We cannot observe internal motives — rewards could reflect strategic signaling rather than pure kindness.","High social desirability in the profile means stated preferences and actual anonymous game behavior might differ (the person might act more prosocially in self-report than in anonymous play).","The mapping from survey traits to specific punishment/reward intensity is probabilistic; some cooperative people do punish free-riders, so assignment is uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.55},{"player":"CHICKEN","probability":0.35},{"player":"PARROT","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["High agreeableness, communion, empathy and generosity in the profile map to consistently high contributions and occasional positive reinforcement — DUCK rewarded others (rounds 1 & 3) and contributed 20 almost every round after an initial lower contribution, reflecting prosocial reinforcement behavior.","High conscientiousness, social-desirability, and a cooperative orientation match CHICKEN’s unwavering 20-coin contributions and minimal chat — steady norm-following cooperation without need for punishment/reward.","PARROT’s near-constant full contributions (one modest deviation at round 24) also match the profile’s strong communal and fair-minded tendencies, though slightly less active in social signaling than DUCK/CHICKEN."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavioral data are limited to contributions and a few reward actions; motives (carefulness vs. misclicks vs. strategic play) are ambiguous.","DUCK’s round-1 low contribution slightly conflicts with a highly conscientious profile — it might be a testing move or reporting noise.","Players with similar contribution patterns (GORILLA, DOG) could also match aspects of the profile (e.g., vocal encouragement), so probabilities reflect some ambiguity."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"SLOTH","probability":0.62},{"player":"DUCK","probability":0.28},{"player":"GORILLA","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently cooperative contribution pattern: SLOTH contributed 20 in 7/8 rounds, matching the profile’s high agreeableness, strong communal orientation, and tendency to be generous in monetary games.","Reward patterns: DUCK frequently rewarded cooperators (including a large reward to SLOTH in round 1) and shows reciprocity, aligning with the subject’s high empathy, high trust-game sending, and preference for fairness.","Balanced cooperation and occasional selective defection: GORILLA cooperated in several rounds and gave modest rewards to multiple players, consistent with a conscientious but pragmatic player (fits the subject’s high conscientiousness and moderate extraversion).","Communication style: SLOTH’s brief, positive chat messages (e.g., \"yes i am\", \"cool\") match a low-extraversion, agreeable participant who is friendly but not highly expressive.","General generosity: The subject’s past-game generosity (ultimatum/dictator/trust metrics) and caregiving self-descriptions correspond best with players who reliably contribute and reward others (SLOTH and DUCK)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Behavior is inferred from in-game contributions, rewards, and brief chat — true motives or real-world demographics are unknown.","Players may be responding to others’ behavior or hidden payoffs not visible in the transcript; similarity judgments assume observed in-game choices reflect stable personality.","Only 8 rounds observed; patterns could change with more observations or different group composition."]}
